title: Disagreement about public information quality and informational price efficiency
abstract: Investors often hold differing opinions on public information quality. This paper shows that such investor disagreement provides a novel explanation for financial market dynamics around earnings announcements. We propose a rational expectations equilibrium model where investors disagree about the precision of a public signal, which separates a pre-news trading period from a post-news trading period. In equilibrium, investor disagreement about public signal precision diminishes informational price efficiency before the news, but enhances it afterward. Consequently, investor disagreement leads to a notable jump in informed trading around the news, a decline in abnormal trading volume before the news and a surge immediately after the news, and underreaction of stock price to announced earnings.

article.1. Introduction: Empirical studies have documented three important financial market dynamics around public news, especially scheduled earnings announcements. The amount of private information incorporated into stock price is significantly higher after earnings announcements than before the announcements (Back et al., 2018; Brennan et al., 2018). In the meantime, the financial market exhibits a pattern of ‚Äúcalm before the storm‚ÄĚ: The abnormal trading volume drops to a negative level right before an earnings announcement but surges substantially immediately after the announcement (Chae, 2005). In addition, after an earnings announcement, stock price usually underreacts to the announced earnings, resulting in the well-known post-earnings-announcement drift (Bernard and Thomas, 1989).This paper shows that investor disagreement about earnings quality provides a novel explanation for the aforementioned observations. Our key insight is as follows. Investors' opinions about earnings quality affect how aggressively they trade on their private signals. Hence, when investors disagree more, the overall trading intensity on private information changes. Such a change will shift the financial market ‚Äúinformational price efficiency,‚ÄĚ which refers to the effectiveness of the financial market in aggregating private signals (Cespa and Vives, 2015). Then, through the interdependence of various financial market outcomes on informational price efficiency, investor disagreement contributes to the dynamics of informed trading and total trading volume around earnings announcements, as well as the price-news sensitivity upon announcements. Importantly, since investors do not observe announced earnings in the pre-announcement period but will trade on them once they are public, investor disagreement oppositely affects pre-announcement and event-period informational price efficiency, resulting in the striking contrast in informed trading and abnormal trading volume between the pre-announcement and the event-period financial markets.Investor disagreement about the quality of a public signal, such as earnings quality, is evident. As direct evidence, Hodge (2003) highlights varied perceptions of earnings quality among non-professional investors in a survey, and Huang et al. (2023) observe that analysts display different skepticism during earnings calls. These studies directly imply that investors do disagree about earnings quality. Their differential opinions may stem from the interaction between firm reporting practices and investor heterogeneity.On one hand, companies engage in earnings management, generating imprecise reports even with auditor endorsement (Nelson et al., 2002). According to Dichev et al. (2013), surveyed CFOs estimate that 50 percent of earnings quality stems from economic fundamentals, with the remaining 50 percent due to managerial reporting discretion. Supporting this, Beyer et al. (2019) find that a significant amount of noise is introduced into earnings quality in the reporting process. In addition, companies may display financial information in a complex manner, leading to data processing disparities among retail investors (Miller, 2010) and among analysts (Sheng and Thevenot, 2015).On the other hand, investor financial sophistication varies, resulting in their differential information processing and thus their heterogeneous opinions about earnings quality. For instance, sophisticated investors are more responsive and attentive to new information (Ben-Rephael et al., 2017), more capable of interpreting complex reports (Miller, 2010), and more skilled at detecting real and accrual-based earnings management (Bushee, 1998; Roychowdhury, 2006; Chung et al., 2002). Furthermore, earnings quality is a multi-dimensional concept that can be assessed using various indicators (Dechow et al., 2010); consequently, investors employing different measures may hold divergent opinions about earnings quality.With the rise of artificial intelligence, the disparity in information processing, and therefore, disagreement about information quality, could further diverge. Computational and resource constraints could increase the wedge between retail and institutional investors. Specializations in processing methods, from high-frequency trading algorithms that takes a split of second to execute to time-consuming deep learning models, could also exacerbate the disagreement among sophisticated investors.Despite the prevalence of investor disagreement about earnings quality, our understanding of how such disagreement influences market dynamics around earnings announcement remains limited. Previous studies emphasize the role of differential interpretations of earnings news, partly attributable to investor disagreement about earnings quality, in determining trading volume upon earnings announcement (Kandel and Pearson, 1995). To delve deeper into the influence of investor disagreement on broader market dynamics surrounding earnings announcement, we develop a rational expectations equilibrium model. Our model comprises two trading periods that are separated by the announcement of a public signal. We focus on a case with one-period-lived investors for analytical tractability. (We also solve a model with long-lived investors and show qualitatively similar investor disagreement effects.) In the model, day-1 investors who trade in the pre-news market cannot observe the public signal and speculate on the day-2 stock price, while day-2 investors observe the public signal and then trade. We assume that each investor has a private signal about the stock value for investigating the dynamics of informed trading.In our model, investors agree to disagree about public signal precision. They hold differential opinions about public signal precision but agree that the opinions of other investors in the same cohort are different and drawn according to an opinion distribution. In addition, investors in the same cohort hold an identical subjective belief about the other trading period's opinion distribution.1 We define that day-t investors' disagreement increases if the day-t opinion distribution shifts to a mean-preserving spread.In equilibrium, an increase in disagreement among day-2 investors enhances post-news informational price efficiency; that is, conditional on all public information, day-2 stock price becomes more informative. This outcome stems from the fact that a day-2 investor's trading intensity on private signal is a decreasing and convex function of her perceived precision of the public signal. Such a decreasing and convex relationship emerges from the process of Bayesian belief updating.At the individual investor level, Bayes' rule implies that as a day-2 investor perceives a more (less) precise public signal, she assigns a lower (greater) weight on the private signal to predict asset's payoff, and consequently, she trades less (more) on her private signal. This is the public signal's crowd-out effect.2At the aggregate level, when day-2 investors disagree more, i.e., when the day-2 opinion distribution undergoes a mean-preserving spread, investors who perceive above-average (below-average) precision in the public signal tend to believe that the public signal is even more (less) precise and thus trade less (more) based on their private signals. However, since trading intensity is a convex function of perceived public signal precision (indicating a diminishing marginal crowd-out effect), the reduction in trading intensity among investors with above-average perceived precision is outweighed by the increase in trading intensity among those with below-average perceived precision. This leads to an overall increase in average trading intensity on private signal, signifying higher post-news informational price efficiency.By contrast, the disagreement among day-1 investors decreases pre-news informational price efficiency. Unlike day-2 investors, day-1 investors do not have access to the public signal; thus, they cannot trade based on it. Consequently, the public signal's crowd-out effect is not applicable in the pre-news period. However, a day-1 investor's perception of public signal precision still influences her trading. Specifically, any day-1 investor understands that the public signal will affect day-2 stock price and that the noise in the public signal constitutes an unlearnable component of her investment return. Therefore, a day-1 investor who perceives a more precise public signal feels less risky investing in the stock, leading to increased trading based on her private signal.At the aggregate level, when day-1 investors disagree more, those who perceive above-average (below-average) precision in the public signal will increase (decrease) trading on their private signals due to increased (decreased) confidence in the public signal. Under mean-variance utility, their trading intensities are increasing and concave functions of their perceived public signal precisions. Consequently, the aggregate increment in the trading intensities of those with above-average perceived public signal precision is dominated by the decrease in the trading intensities of those with below-average perceived public signal precision, resulting in a lower aggregate trading intensity on private signal and a less informative stock price.While we focus on short-lived investors for analytical tractability, we show that the effects of investor disagreement are qualitatively similar in a model with long-lived investors. In such a model, investors trade in both periods, and their subjective beliefs about public signal precision remain unchanged before and after the announcement of the public signal. Different from the short-lived investor case, long-lived investors may trade for hedging purposes in the pre-news period. Despite this difference, we characterize the equilibrium of the model and find qualitatively similar effects of investor disagreement.The contrasting effects of investor disagreement on pre-news and post-news informational price efficiency provide a novel explanation for the financial market dynamics around earnings announcements. First, informational price efficiency results from investors' trading intensity on their private signals, so high informational price efficiency is indicative of substantial informed trading. Consequently, investor disagreement contributes to the significant jump in informed trading following an earnings announcement, as documented by Back et al. (2018) and Brennan et al. (2018).3Second, the pattern of abnormal trading volume decreasing to a negative level before earnings announcement and surging substantially upon the announcement, as shown by Chae (2005), can also be attributed to investor disagreement. Indeed, the dip in the abnormal trading volume before earnings announcements has become even stronger in recent years (Sammon, 2022, Figure E.2). In our model with investor disagreement, the total trading volume can be decomposed into a disagreement trading component and an informed trading component. While investor disagreement always increases disagreement-driven trading, it increases informed trading upon announcement but reduces informed trading before announcement. Our numerical analysis shows that in the pre-news trading period, the informed trading component dominates the disagreement trading component, so investor disagreement has an overall negative effect on the pre-news trading volume. Hence, with investor disagreement, the abnormal trading volume dips prior to an earnings announcement and surges upon the announcement.Finally, investor disagreement also affects stock price reaction to announced earnings. In the post-news trading period, investor disagreement leads to higher informational price efficiency, providing investors a more informative price signal. Such a price signal will then ‚Äúcrowd out‚ÄĚ the public signal's effect on stock price. Therefore, as day-2 investors disagree more, the stock price is less sensitive to the public signal. This result indicates that investor disagreement could contribute to stock price underreaction to announced earnings (Bernard and Thomas, 1989).4These are all testable predictions.5 However, we notice that any single phenomenon above cannot identify investor disagreement about earnings quality, since other types of investor heterogeneity may have the same prediction. We nevertheless show that other types of investor heterogeneity, such as differential priors, dispersed private information precision, and disagreement about public signal bias, cannot generate all the three predictions. Hence, the aforementioned financial market dynamics as a whole are likely to identify investor disagreement about earnings quality, helping one better understand the nature of investor heterogeneity.There is an extant literature on investor disagreement. Differences-of-opinion models typically start out with either different priors or different expectations of a public signal.6 In particular, Banerjee and Kremer (2010) study the investor disagreement about the mean of public signals and volume dynamics. While their model also predicts jumps in trading volume around earnings announcement, their focus is on the relationship between the frequency of investor disagreement and the long-run serial correlation in volume and volatility. While modeling investor disagreement about the mean of a public signal has delivered fruitful results, it may not directly help analyze informational price efficiency and may not explain all three phenomena of financial market dynamics around earnings announcements this paper focuses on. Few papers in this literature, such as Kandel and Pearson (1995) and Cao and Ouyang (2009), model investor disagreement on public signal precision. Kandel and Pearson (1995) analyze investors' differential belief updating around earnings announcement to explain the abnormal trading volume documented for certain firms experiencing close-to-zero price reactions. Cao and Ouyang (2009) study how investor disagreement about public signals could have different effects on stock trading and option trading upon announcements. These papers mainly focus on trading volume upon the release of public information and assume that investors do not have private signals.7We highlight the effects of investors' diverse perceptions of the precision of a public signal on the trading on their private signals.8 Thereby, we contribute to the literature by showing that through affecting informational price efficiency, investor disagreement about earnings quality provides explanation for dynamics of informed trading and volume around earnings announcement, as well as price underreaction to earnings announcement. In addition, allowing investors to possess private signals generates different predictions from the literature. For example, investors' disagreement about future public signal precision leads them to trade in the pre-news period, while Cao and Ouyang (2009) find that investor disagreement about future public signal precision does not generate trading for stocks without options.More broadly, our paper contributes to the literature on information aggregation and informational price efficiency. Information aggregation plays a key role in asset pricing in financial markets (Hayek, 1945). Central to such aggregation is investors' utilization of information available to them and importantly, this information includes the equilibrium price (Grossman and Stiglitz, 1980; Glosten and Milgrom, 1985; Kyle, 1985). When some of the investors have short investment horizons, they do not use their information to form expectations about the realized fundamental value of a long-lived asset. Instead, they use it to form expectations of the intermediate price at which they will liquidate their asset holding (Allen et al., 2006). This paper contributes to this literature by analyzing how investor disagreement about public signal precision affects the ability of the financial market in aggregating investor private information.

Disagreement and financial market dyn: Having characterized an equilibrium of the model, we are now in a position to analyze how investor disagreement about the public signal precision affects the financial market. In Section 4.1 and Section 4.2, we analytically discuss how investor disagreement affects informational price efficiency in the post-news and pre-news financial markets, respectively.The analysis about informational price efficiency is not only our theoretical focus in this paper but also provides insights into financial market dynamics around public news. Building upon the analytical results about informational price efficiency, we study broader asset pricing implications of disagreement in Section 4.3. In particular, we focus on the effects of investor disagreement on dynamics of informed trading and total trading volume, as well as price-news sensitivity. We argue that these implications provide potential explanations for documented empirical observations.4.1. Post-news informational price efficiencyIn this subsection, we analyze how day-2 investor disagreement affects post-news informational price efficiency. We consider an exogenous shift of day-2 investors' opinion distribution, from F2 to a mean-preserving spread G2. Note that such a shift of the day-2 investor opinion distribution does not change Fňú2, the day-1 investors' subjective belief about the day-2 opinion distribution. Consequently, the day-1 pricing function remains unchanged. Hence, the question is formulated as how day-2 price informativeness z2 changes when F2 shifts to G2.The day-2 equilibrium characterization equation (14) shows that z2 is determined by day-2 investors' average trading intensity on their private signals. Therefore, we first analyze how the perceived public signal precision affects an individual day-2 investor's demand, and then derive the effect of the belief distribution shift by aggregating these demand changes.From a day-2 investor i's stock demand function (11), her perceived public signal precision őĪ2i affects her trading intensity in two ways. First, őĪ2i affects how investor i updates her belief about the stock fundamentals őł. As shown in equation (12), a larger őĪ2i makes investor i rely more on the public signal y but less on her private signal x2i to estimate the stock value. This is the public signal's crowd-out effect, which is represented by ‚ąāE[v|F2i;őĪ2i]/‚ąāx2i. Second, an increase in őĪ2i increases investor i's information precision, reducing her risk of holding a non-zero position in the stock and making her trade more on her private signal. This can be seen from a smaller posterior variance (13) and is thus called a risk-reducing effect.When the stock's terminal value v has no residual uncertainty (ŌĄ‚ąí1=0), the crowd-out effect and the risk-reducing effect of a change in őĪ2i just cancel each other out. In such a case, any day-2 investor's opinion on the public signal precision will not affect her trading intensity on her private signal. Such a null effect at the individual level then implies a null effect of the opinion distribution on informational price efficiency (at the aggregate level). We summarize this result in Proposition 3. Proposition 3If there is no residual uncertainty of the stock value, that is, if ŌĄ‚ąí1=0, then ‚Āéz2‚Āé=ŌĀő≤, which is independent of F2.With residual uncertainty, there is a component in investor i's posterior variance that does not decrease with őĪ2i, so the risk-reducing effect becomes weaker and is dominated by the crowd-out effect. Then, day-2 investors' opinion distribution will affect day-2 informational price efficiency. Indeed, the crowd-out effect suggests that investors who perceive higher public signal precision trade less on their private signals. Bayesian belief updating further implies that the marginal crowd-out effect is diminishing. article.6. Empirical implications: We discuss the empirical implications of our theoretical analysis in this section. In Section 4 and Section 5, we show that with short-lived investors or with long-lived investors, investor disagreement about earnings quality significantly affects the informational price efficiency and thus influences the financial market dynamics. Such effects lead to testable cross-sectional predictions, which are summarized in Prediction 1.Prediction 1Earnings announcements with greater investor disagreement about earnings quality are associated with(a)greater jump of informed trading between the pre-announcement period and the event period;(b)lower abnormal trading volume before the announcement but higher abnormal trading volume upon the announcement;(c)weaker immediate price reactions to earnings news and stronger post-earnings-announcement drifts.We perform preliminary empirical tests of Prediction 1. We first employ a commonly used metric for differential interpretation of earnings news ‚Äď the standard deviation of analysts' revisions. This measure encapsulates various elements of belief heterogeneity, such as differential interpretations by analysts regarding the mean and precision of a public signal. To construct a more plausible measure of disagreement about earnings quality, we build a simple Bayesian learning model in which analysts make both annual earnings forecasts and quarterly earnings forecasts. When quarterly earnings news is announced, analysts compare the announced earnings to their pre-announcement forecasts and use these differences to update their forecasts regarding annual earnings. Then, we compute the ratio of an analyst's annual earnings forecast revision following an interim quarterly earnings announcement, relative to the difference between the announced quarterly earnings and the analyst's pre-announcement quarterly earnings forecast. According to Bayes' rule, this ratio reflects the weight that the analyst assigns to the public signal when updating her belief. Consequently, the standard deviation of this ratio is a plausible measure of disagreement about earnings quality.We find supporting evidence, which is presented in an Online Appendix along with details on variables constructions.12 Our findings imply that greater disagreement is associated with reduced abnormal turnover in the pre-announcement window and increased abnormal turnover upon announcement. We also observe weaker immediate price reactions to earnings news within the two-day announcement window, and there is partial support of a stronger post-earnings announcement drift for up to 40 trading days.It is important to point out that any of these predictions in isolation is consistent with but not a test of our model ‚Äď other models can yield similar results through different mechanisms. However, the combination of these predictions, especially parts (b) and (c), is likely to distinctly identify investor disagreement about earnings quality. As we illustrate in Section 7, heterogeneous private information precision, heterogeneous priors, and differential interpretations of the mean of the public signal yield different predictions of volume and price reactions, which mitigates concerns that our empirical results are influenced by alternative belief heterogeneities inadvertently captured by our imperfect measures.Moreover, the fact that different types of belief heterogeneities yield different price and volume dynamics can actually help disentangle and uncover crucial sources of investor disagreement. While the effects of investor disagreement on the financial market have been extensively studied, the sources of investor disagreement remain largely unexplored (Cookson and Niessner, 2020). These sources include investors' heterogeneity in prior beliefs, their different interpretations of the announced earnings, and their varied private information precision, but there is a lack of consensus on which aspect is more important (Bamber et al., 1997, Bamber et al., 1999; Atiase et al., 2016). We demonstrate in Section 7 that the three predictions in Prediction 1 collectively are unique to our model and cannot be explained by any other type of investor heterogeneity. Thereby, we offer a potential identification of various types of disagreement based on these predictions.In addition to offering testable predictions, our model also links economic factors that may affect investor disagreement about earnings quality to financial market dynamics. As we argued, investor disagreement about earnings quality results from the interaction between firms' reporting practices and investors' diverse abilities and financial literacy. Consequently, firms that disclose more complex reports or have investors with a wide range of information processing capabilities are likely to experience more varied opinions on earnings quality.13 These firms, therefore, are more likely to experience the financial market dynamics outlined in Prediction 1.Yet, we acknowledge that neither the measures used in our preliminary empirical tests nor the economic factors considered constitute direct measures of investor disagreement, as they might capture effects beyond investor disagreement. For instance, the readability of financial disclosure impacts not only the dispersion of perceived earnings quality among investors, but also its average. This could confound any identified associations with price or volume dynamics. Such limitations underscore the need for more direct measures of investor disagreement.While the development of such a measure is beyond the scope of this paper, we propose potential approaches. In our opinion, textual analysis of market participants' expressed opinions on earnings news could provide fruitful insights. One approach might be to adopt the methodology developed by Huang et al. (2023) to identify skepticism expressed in analysts' questions during earnings calls. Given that skepticism is directly and inversely correlated with analysts' confidence in earnings quality, variations in skepticism could potentially serve as a direct measure of investor disagreement about earnings quality. In addition to analyzing analysts' questions, one could also examine investor comments on platforms such as StockTwits, Reddit, or Twitter. These social media platforms provide a wealth of unfiltered opinions that could further illuminate the nature and extent of investor disagreement.
article.7. Robustness: We have shown that the disagreement effects are robust with respect to the trading horizon of investors. In this section, we examine whether alternative types of investor heterogeneity can explain our results. We also extend the model to incorporate endogenous information acquisition. We include all the proofs in an Online Appendix.Disagreement about day-2 opinion distribution.‚ÄÉ In the model described in Section 2, day-1 investors agree on the day-2 opinion distribution (i.e., they have a common perceived day-2 opinion distribution Fňú2). Unlike the disagreement about the mean of the public signal, heterogeneity in private signal precision directly affects private trading and equilibrium prices. Hence, a mean-preserving spread of the distribution of ő≤2i leads to less informational price efficiency and lower informed trading upon a public announcement, which is opposite to the effects of disagreement about public news precision. However, for given distributions of investor private signal precision, the effects of investor disagreement on financial market dynamics are qualitatively same as those in Section 4.3.Endogenous information acquisition.‚ÄÉ In our baseline models, investors are endowed with private signals of homogeneous precision. In practice, however, investors' disagreement about the quality of a public signal may lead them to endogenously acquire private signals with heterogeneous qualities. Since the qualities of investors' private signals will determine informational price efficiency, endogenous private information acquisition could be a new channel through which investor disagreement about public signal precision affects informational price efficiency.In the Online Appendix, we analyze the effects of investor disagreement about public signal quality on informational price efficiency, when investors endogenously acquire private signals. We show that endogenous private information acquisition reinforces the crowd-out and the crowd-in effects in the post-news and the pre-news financial markets, respectively. Therefore, the conclusions we draw from our baseline model are robust.
